FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data release 2.0.1; 21 U.S. sites; 9-10 year-old children at baseline; confirmed U.S. ABCD cohort throughout methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ML predictive analysis using elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting, and SVR to predict delayed reward discounting (DRD) one year later from baseline ABCD data (N=4042).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is defined as 'weekly average hours a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' (p.17) — a multi-device aggregate bundling computer, tablet, and cellphone without per-device breakout anywhere in the paper. Step 1 finds no phone/SM-specific estimate (Table 3 lists 'Weekend Screen Time (parent report)', 'Weekday Screen Time (youth report)', 'Weekend Screen Time (parent report)' as multi-device aggregates). Step 2 fails: composite includes non-phone ingredients (computer, tablet) without separate breakout. FT-IC2 binding rule 5 applies.

notes: Screen time measure explicitly defined as multi-device aggregate (computer + cellphone + tablet + other electronic device) per methods p.17, citing Paulus et al. 2019. No per-device or phone-only breakout found in main tables, Table 3, or discussion of supplementary analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional baseline data from the ABCD study, n=10,650 children aged 9.9 years, recruited by probability sampling at 21 U.S. sites (2016-2018 data release).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multilevel general linear models and logistic regression examining parental psychological problems and children's screen media behaviors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Acta Paediatrica 2022; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports separate numeric estimates (regression coefficients with 95% CI) for children's social media use (minutes/day) as DV, paired with parental externalizing/internalizing psychopathology (individual-trait-level parental psychopathology) as IV. E.g., internalizing model 1: coefficient=0.01 (95%CI -3.80, 3.77); externalizing model 1: coefficient=2.17 (95%CI -2.25, 6.59). Social media is a qualifying phone/SM variable; parental psychopathology is individual-trait-level on the other side.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric regression coefficients (with 95% CI) reported for social media use across all parental problem models in Table 3; additional logistic regression results for binary social media use reported in text (p-values 0.201-0.786).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses conducted pairing parental psychopathology with children's social media use; numeric estimates reported (Table 3, Table 4, Table 2).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design. This paper is entirely cross-sectional (explicitly stated in conclusion: 'This cross-sectional study was not able to examine causal associations'). FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: SM (social media time minutes/day) analyzed as separate DV in Table 3, with parental externalizing/internalizing/total problems as IV. No significant associations found but numeric estimates are present. The paper also reports separate analyses for TV, videos, games, mature-rated media as additional DVs.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from ABCD Study release 3.0; 21 U.S. recruitment sites; N=6749 adolescents primarily aged 12-13; COVID RRR Survey 1 (May 2020) linked to baseline and follow-up data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear regression to estimate associations between ACE score and screen time and physical activity during COVID-19 pandemic (May 2020).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics 2022; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is a sum of 7 modalities: 'multiple-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching or streaming movies, videos, or television shows' (p.1295). Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found; only the total composite is modeled as DV. Step 2 fails: composite includes TV/movie streaming, single-player gaming (unspecified platform), multiple-player gaming (unspecified platform), and internet browsing — all non-phone/non-SM ingredients bundled without separate breakout. FT-IC2 binding rule 5 applies (multi-device/multi-activity aggregate with no phone-specific isolation).

notes: Although texting, social media, and video chatting are among the 7 components of the screen time composite, the composite also includes TV/movie streaming and unspecified-platform gaming, and no breakout coefficients for individual modalities are reported. ACEs (individual-trait) is the IV; composite screen time is the DV.
